title: gno tracking issue
author: benluelo
body:
- [ ] figure out how to query unbonding period from the chain
- [ ] add tx simulation once it's implemented upstream
- [ ] add more types to the gno-types crate
  - [ ] find a canonical source for protobuf definitions generated from amino
- [ ] implement PacketsByBatchHash query (needed for batch commitments)
- [ ] add better helper methods to gno-client for the various different abci queries (`vm/qeval`, `vm/qeval_json`, `vm/docs`, `vm/types`, etc)
- [ ] add codec smoke tests to the gno-client for all endpoints
- [ ] add trivial event filtering to the gno event source plugin
- [ ] implement the rest of the events handling in the gno event source plugin
- [ ] add gno implementation to https://docs.union.build/connect/implementations/
- [ ] add more tests to gno lc

title: Add Makefile for building "uniond" without a Nix toolchain
author: afa-farkhod
body:
## Summary

The repository currently has no `Makefile`. Every build target is expressed
through `flake.nix`, which is the correct approach for hermetic, reproducible
CI and release builds. However, this creates a meaningful barrier for node
operators, validators, and developers who want to build and run `uniond` using
the standard Go toolchain — the same way every other Cosmos SDK-based chain
works today.

## Problem

Virtually every production Cosmos SDK node (Osmosis, Juno, Celestia, dYdX,
Neutron, Babylon, etc.) ships a `Makefile` that exposes:

```
make build    # compile the binary
make install  # install to $GOPATH/bin
```

Operators rely on this muscle memory. When onboarding to a new chain, the
first thing a validator does is clone the repo and run `make install`. The
absence of a `Makefile` in Union's repo creates immediate confusion and
friction:

1. **No standard entry point.** There is no obvious way to build `uniond`
   without reading `flake.nix` and understanding the Nix ecosystem.

2. **Nix is a hard prerequisite.** Installing Nix is a multi-step process
   with system-level implications. Many infrastructure teams run hardened
   servers (RHEL, Ubuntu LTS, Debian) where installing Nix is not permitted
   or practical.

3. **CI/CD pipeline integration.** Most validators run custom CI pipelines
   (Jenkins, GitLab CI, internal tooling) that invoke `make build` or
   `make install`. These pipelines cannot easily be adapted to call
   `nix build .#uniond` without a full Nix installation.

4. **Documentation and tutorials.** The vast majority of Cosmos validator
   guides, blog posts, and runbooks reference `make install`. Union's docs
   will stand alone in requiring a fundamentally different build flow.

5. **Developer onboarding.** New contributors who want to modify and locally
   test `uniond` face an unnecessary first hurdle before they can even
   compile the project.

## Why a Makefile Is Not Redundant Here

The repo is a polyglot Nix monorepo, and `flake.nix` correctly handles the
full build graph across Rust, TypeScript, Solidity, Cairo, and Go. A
`Makefile` does **not** replace that — it serves a different audience:

| Audience | Tool |
|---|---|
| CI, release, reproducible builds | `nix build .#uniond` (unchanged) |
| Validators / node operators | `make install` |
| Developers iterating locally | `make build` |
| Docker / custom pipelines | `make build` → copy binary |

The two approaches coexist without conflict. The `Makefile` is a thin,
well-documented wrapper around `go build` with the correct CGO flags,
build tags, and ldflags that match the Nix build.

## Proposed Solution

Add a `Makefile` at the repository root that provides:

| Target | Description |
|---|---|
| `make build` | Compile `uniond` to `./build/uniond` |
| `make install` | Install `uniond` to `$GOPATH/bin` |
| `make get-libwasmvm` | Download the `libwasmvm` static/shared library (one-time setup) |
| `make test` | Run Go unit tests in `uniond/` |
| `make lint` | Run `golangci-lint` |
| `make clean` | Remove `./build/` artifacts |
| `make nix-build` | Delegate to `nix build .#uniond` (preserved canonical path) |
| `make help` | Print all available targets |

### Key implementation details

- **`libwasmvm` dependency:** `uniond` links against
  `github.com/CosmWasm/wasmvm/v2 v2.2.1`. A `get-libwasmvm` target
  downloads the correct pre-built artifact from the CosmWasm GitHub
  releases (`libwasmvm_muslc.{arch}.a` on Linux,
  `libwasmvm.{arch}.dylib` on macOS) and caches it under `./build/lib/`.
  The `build` and `install` targets depend on it, so it is fetched
  automatically on first use.

- **Build tags match Nix:** Linux builds use `netgo,muslc` tags and static
  linking (`-linkmode external -extldflags "-static"`), matching the Nix
  output exactly. macOS uses dynamic linking against the downloaded dylib.

- **Version metadata injected via ldflags:** `VERSION` and `COMMIT` are
  derived from `git describe` / `git rev-parse` and injected into the
  `cosmos-sdk/version` package, matching the release build.

- **Platform safety:** The Makefile detects Linux vs. macOS and selects
  the appropriate library and flags. An unsupported platform emits a clear
  error pointing to `nix build .#uniond`.

## Expected Impact

- Node operators can follow the exact same onboarding flow as every other
  Cosmos SDK chain.
- The canonical Nix build path (`flake.nix`) is completely unaffected.
- External tutorials, validator guides, and automation scripts work
  out-of-the-box.
- New contributors have a zero-friction path to build the node locally.

title: [DIAGNOSTIC] Configuration mismatch: Default rpc.max_body_bytes causes synchronization failure
author: Cripto5588
body:
Summary
A configuration mismatch has been identified between default RPC settings and current Light Client (LC) requirements. The default rpc.max_body_bytes = 1048576 (1MB) in config.toml causes synchronization failure in default setups, as observed LC payloads exceeded this threshold during synchronization tests.

2. Environment & Reproduction
Target: union-nodes (mainnet/testnet binaries)

Config Location: config.toml#L45 and app.toml (rpc-max-body-bytes)

Reproduction Steps:

Start a node using the default config.toml.

Attempt an LC upload with a payload > 1MB.

Observe the immediate HTTP 413 rejection.

Increase rpc.max_body_bytes to 10MB and restart.

Retry the same payload; the upload succeeds.

3. Evidence (Diagnostic Log)
The following trace confirms that the rejection occurs at the transport layer, meaning the request is discarded before the consensus logic or proof parser can process it.

Plaintext
--- TEST: PAYLOAD > 1MB (REPRODUCING DEFAULT CONFIG FAILURE) ---
> POST /rpc HTTP/1.1
> Content-Length: 2097152
< HTTP/1.1 100 Continue
< HTTP/1.1 413 REQUEST ENTITY TOO LARGE
4. Technical Analysis
Operational Mismatch: Observed payloads during synchronization reached ~2MB, rendering the 1MB default obsolete for the current protocol state.

Transport Layer Blocking: Because the failure returns a standard HTTP 413, the node rejects the proof during the initial handshake. This prevents the request from ever reaching the handler or being logged as a protocol error, making it difficult for operators to diagnose without low-level network inspection.

Outcome: Nodes operating under default configurations are unable to synchronize until manual intervention is performed on the configuration files.

title: The U drop page on the app.union.build glitches
author: Ramseyyy10
body:
The airdrop eligibility page is glitching and marking valid users as ineligible.

✅ Steps to Reproduce
1. Go to the [Unionlabs airdrop eligibility page] (insert link here).
2. Connect a valid wallet that meets eligibility criteria.
3. Observe that the system incorrectly displays “Not Eligible”.

Expected Behavior
Users who meet all criteria should be recognized as eligible for the airdrop.

Actual Behavior
* Real users with valid wallets are being flagged as ineligible.
* This prevents them from claiming rewards.

📷 Screenshots
￼

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c47ff1e8-f090-4bbf-aecf-e85a2521c794)

* Device: (e.g., MacBook Air
* Browser: (Chrome v127 )
* Wallet: (Metamask & Rabby)

Additional Context
This issue could impact community trust and fairness in the airdrop distribution. It may be related to:
* Eligibility check logic
* Wallet verification API
* Frontend caching/glitch

title: Feature request: Remember last bridged asset value for easier repeat bridging
author: HusseinAdeiza
body:
## Problem
When testing the Union bridge protocol on testnet, I observed that when a user bridges assets, the input value is not retained for subsequent bridges. This means users must re-enter the same value if they wish to bridge multiple times with the same asset amount. This can create friction, especially for users who frequently bridge the same value.

## Proposed Feature
Add a feature that remembers the last entered asset value for bridging. When a user completes a bridge transaction, the UI should retain the previously inputted value, so users can easily repeat the same bridge with the same amount. This would:
- Reduce repetitive manual entry for frequent bridging
- Improve user experience and efficiency
- Make batch/multi-bridging more convenient

## Usage Example
1. User initiates a bridge with value 'X'.
2. After completion, the input field retains value 'X'.
3. User can initiate another bridge with the same value without re-entering it.

## Context
Requested during testnet by tester ID: ca1e76b3-f536-4880-9203-4d7f6ac7802d. This feature would be beneficial for both app.union.build and for the mainnet experience
